content:
8 min read
Just now

--

I’ve always been precious about my mornings.
Coffee before screens. A slow read. Maybe ten minutes of stretching that I’d convinced myself counted as exercise. My morning routine wasn’t optimized — it was mine. Quiet, slightly inefficient, deeply human.
Then I agreed to spend 30 days handing it over to AI.
Not as a stunt. Not because I thought AI would make me a better person. I did it because I was genuinely curious whether the tools I’d been recommending to clients — productivity apps, AI assistants, automated schedulers — actually held up when I stopped demo-ing them and started living with them before 9 AM.
Here’s the honest answer.
What the Routine Looked Like Before
My old morning ran about 90 minutes, give or take:
Wake up around 6:30. Make coffee. Scroll through email for 20 minutes while pretending I wasn’t. Read a few articles. Write in a paper journal — nothing structured, just whatever surfaced. Review my task list. Head to my desk by 8:00.
Productive? Loosely. Intentional? Not really. But it worked well enough that I’d never questioned it.
That was the point of the experiment. I wasn’t trying to fix something broken. I wanted to find out what happens when you apply serious tools to a process that was only ever good enough.
The AI Stack I Used
I kept the tools accessible — nothing that required a developer setup or a premium subscription most people wouldn’t already have.
ChatGPT (GPT-4o) — my primary thinking partner. Used for morning briefings, journaling prompts, and decision-making support.
Notion AI — for task prioritization and daily planning inside the workspace I was already using.
Reclaim.ai — an AI scheduling tool that auto-blocks time on my calendar based on my priorities and energy patterns.
Otter.ai — for voice journaling. Instead of writing, I talked. Otter transcribed, then I fed the transcript to ChatGPT for reflection.
Perplexity AI — replacing my morning article scroll with AI-curated summaries of topics I’d set as ongoing interests.
That was the stack. Five tools, 30 days, every morning.
Week One: Fascinating and Exhausting
The first thing I noticed wasn’t efficiency. It was friction.
Setting up the tools properly — teaching Reclaim my actual priorities, calibrating Perplexity’s topic interests, establishing a consistent voice journaling habit with Otter — took real effort. The first three days I spent more time configuring than I saved. I went to my desk later than usual, mildly stressed, wondering if I’d already failed the experiment.
By day five, things started clicking. Reclaim had learned enough about my calendar patterns to start blocking deep work windows I hadn’t asked for but genuinely needed. My Perplexity briefing — five topics, ten minutes — replaced 25 minutes of aimless scrolling with something focused. I felt informed rather than anxious, which is not how my previous news consumption had been going.
The journaling shift was the strangest. Talking instead of writing felt unnatural at first — like leaving a voicemail for yourself. But by day six I noticed I was saying things I wouldn’t have written. The informality of speech unlocked a different kind of honesty. When ChatGPT reflected the transcript back with follow-up questions, a few of those questions were genuinely uncomfortable. In a useful way.
"Talking instead of writing felt like leaving a voicemail for yourself — and then that voicemail asked you questions you weren’t ready for."
Week Two: The Efficiency Kicks In
Week two was when I started understanding what the tools were actually good at.
Notion AI’s task prioritization saved me from a habit I hadn’t named until it disappeared: the morning fake-productivity spiral. I’d open my task list, feel overwhelmed, rearrange things without actually deciding anything, and spend 15 minutes achieving the illusion of organization. Notion AI, given my tasks and a simple prompt about my top goal for the week, would surface a ranked list in about 40 seconds. It was right roughly 80% of the time. The other 20%, I’d override it — but even then, having something to argue against was faster than starting from zero.
The Perplexity briefings had genuinely changed my information diet. Instead of the ambient anxiety of a Twitter or LinkedIn scroll, I was getting structured summaries on AI developments, industry news in my field, and one wildcard topic I rotated weekly — behavioral economics, urban planning, whatever I was curious about. I felt less reactive and more curious in my conversations. People noticed.
The ChatGPT planning session — a five-minute exchange where I described my day and asked for a structured plan — was the sleeper hit of the experiment. Not because the plans were brilliant, but because articulating my day out loud (even in text) before starting it changed how I entered it. It’s the same reason rubber duck debugging works. Explaining something, even to a patient, non-judgmental AI, clarifies it.
Week Three: The Cracks Appear
I want to be honest about week three, because most 30-day experiment essays skip it.
The novelty had worn off. The tools were working, in the narrow sense — my mornings were more structured, my planning more deliberate, my information consumption more focused. But something else was happening that I hadn’t anticipated.
I was spending my mornings in a kind of managed efficiency that left no room for the wandering that had quietly been doing important work in my old routine.
The 20-minute coffee-and-scroll I’d been dismissing as waste? Some of my best ideas had come from it. Not from the scrolling itself, but from the associative, undirected mental state it created — the cognitive equivalent of looking out a window. Reclaim’s perfectly optimized schedule had no slot for looking out windows.
I also noticed I’d stopped writing full sentences in my journal. The voice-to-text-to-AI pipeline was faster and in some ways richer, but the slow physical act of writing had forced a kind of patience that the new system didn’t. I’d been writing faster but thinking, possibly, less deeply.
"Reclaim’s perfectly optimized schedule had no slot for looking out windows."
Week Four: Finding the Balance
By week four I’d started making small modifications. This felt like cheating at first — the experiment was supposed to be a full replacement, not a negotiation. But I realized the negotiation was itself the finding.
I reintroduced ten minutes of unstructured morning time before engaging any AI tool. Just coffee and silence. No Perplexity briefing, no ChatGPT session — just sitting. Reclaim could schedule from 7:15 onward. The first fifteen minutes were mine, deliberately empty.
Immediately, the rest of the AI-assisted morning worked better. It was as if the unstructured opening created a mental baseline that made the structured efficiency feel useful rather than imposed.
I also dropped Otter from my daily routine and kept it for occasional use. The voice journaling had been valuable to try, but it hadn’t replaced what written journaling did for me. Some things don’t have better AI alternatives — they just have different ones.
What the Numbers Said
Over 30 days, I tracked a few things consistently:
Time to desk: Average of 7:52 AM with AI routine vs. 8:06 AM before. Saved roughly 14 minutes per morning.
Tasks completed by noon: Up from an average of 3.2 to 4.1 meaningful tasks. A 28% improvement, though "meaningful" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
Self-reported focus score (1–10): 7.1 average during AI month vs. 6.4 in the month prior.
Self-reported mood score (1–10): 6.8 during AI month vs. 7.2 before. Lower. Notably.
That last number is the one I keep returning to. More productive, measurably. Slightly less happy about it. Whether that trade-off is worth it probably depends on what you’re optimizing for — and whether you’ve actually decided what that is.
What AI Did Really Well
Eliminating decision fatigue early. The morning is when willpower is highest and decision fatigue is lowest. AI tools are extremely good at absorbing low-stakes decisions — what to read, in what order to do things, when to schedule what — so that mental energy is preserved for decisions that actually matter.
Providing structure without judgment. There’s something genuinely useful about a planning tool that doesn’t get frustrated, doesn’t have a mood, and doesn’t bring its own agenda to your morning. The neutrality of AI assistance, which can feel cold in other contexts, is an asset in early-morning planning.
Accelerating information triage. Perplexity’s briefings were genuinely better than my own scrolling at surfacing relevant information quickly. Not deeper — but faster and less emotionally manipulative than social feeds designed to trigger engagement.
What AI Did Poorly
Creating space for genuine insight. The efficiency gains were real, but insight — the unexpected connection, the thing that reframes a problem — tends to come from unstructured time, not optimized time. The AI morning was better at executing on existing priorities than generating new ones.
Replacing embodied habits. Physical journaling, slow coffee, the specific texture of a quiet house before the day starts — these are not productivity inputs. They are what make productivity sustainable. No tool replaced them well.
Knowing when to do nothing. This is the deepest limitation. AI tools are optimizers. Some mornings you don’t need optimization — you need rest, or play, or a long shower where you solve the problem you didn’t know you had. The stack had no mode for that.
Would I Recommend It?
Yes, with conditions.
If your mornings are genuinely chaotic — if you’re constantly reactive, constantly behind, losing hours to scrolling and fake-productivity spirals — an AI-assisted morning routine will help you significantly. The structure it imposes is real and the time it saves is real.
If your mornings are already working — if you have a baseline of intentionality and you’re looking for marginal gains — the tools will help at the edges but might quietly erode something you didn’t know you were valuing.
The version I’d actually recommend isn’t the one I tested: full replacement for 30 days. It’s a hybrid — AI for planning, prioritization, and information briefing; human space for the first few minutes, for journaling in whatever form suits you, and for the occasional morning where you override the schedule entirely and do something the algorithm wouldn’t suggest.
The best morning routine is probably one where AI handles what AI is good at, and you protect the parts it isn’t.
Five AI Morning Tools Worth Trying
1. ChatGPT or Claude for daily planning. Five-minute conversation: your top goal, your constraints, your energy level. Ask for a structured plan. Override 20% of it. Ship.
2. Perplexity AI for news. Set five topic areas. Read the briefing. Close the tab. Infinitely better than a social scroll.
3. Reclaim.ai for calendar blocking. Particularly useful if you manage your own schedule and have chronic difficulty protecting deep work time.
4. Notion AI or similar for task ranking. Paste your task list, describe your week’s priority, ask what to do first. Fast and usually correct.
5. Voice journaling + AI reflection. Talk for five minutes, paste the transcript into ChatGPT, ask for three patterns it noticed. Uncomfortable and useful in roughly equal measure.
The Honest Conclusion
I went into this experiment expecting to discover that AI morning routines were either transformative or overhyped. What I found was more nuanced: they’re genuinely useful in specific ways, quietly costly in others, and best deployed as a layer on top of human habits rather than a replacement for them.
The 30 days changed how I use my mornings. Not by proving that AI was the answer, but by showing me, precisely, what I’d been doing before that I hadn’t known I was valuing.
Sometimes the best thing an experiment can do is teach you to appreciate what you had.
